titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Committee on the Civil War Memorial, Yale University, Records
Committee on the Civil War Memorial, Yale University, Records
titlestmt.author: compiled by Laura Watt

controlnote.p: This encoded finding aid is compliant with the Yale EAD Best Practice Guidelines, Version 1.0.
This collection contains 15 published digital objects. And those objects are comprised of 265 digital files.

descriptivenote.p: Finding aid description and metadata in Archives at Yale are licensed under a CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. This means that anyone may copy, modify, and distribute this descriptive metadata without restriction or permission. The CC0 license is explained in full on the Creative Commons website: CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. More information is available on our Help page.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Thursday August 21, 2025 at 03:04

did.unittitle: Committee on the Civil War Memorial, Yale University, records
did.abstract: The records consist of correspondence, reports, and clippings documenting the activities of the Yale Committee on the Civil War Memorial.

corpname.part: Yale University. Committee on the Civil War Memorial

odd.head: General note
odd.p: Forms part of Yale Record Group 6 (YRG 6), Records of Yale committees.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Committee on the Civil War Memorial, Yale University, Records (RU 529). Manuscripts and Archives, Yale University Library.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: The materials are open for research.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: The records are arranged by accession.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The records consist of correspondence, reports, and clippings documenting the activities of the Yale Committee on the Civil War Memorial. Yale's Civil War Memorial is located in a prominent corridor of Memorial Hall and was dedicated in 1915. It lists the names and affiliations of Yale alumni who died in the Civil War, including both those who fought for the Union and for the Confederacy.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: The committee appointed to plan the memorial was chaired by two members of the Yale College class of 1854: Henry E. Howland, who had fought for the Union during the war, and William W. Gordon, who had fought for the Confederacy. Other members of the committee were Simeon Eben Baldwin, Henry P. Wright, Talcott Russell, Charles H. Strong, Frank L. Polk, Anson Phelps Stokes, Jr., Lee McClung, Henry V. Freeman, Allen W. Evarts, William W. Gordon, Jr., and Thomas West. United States President William Howard Taft presided over the 1909 alumni meeting at which it was decided to construct the memorial.
Information in this historical note was drawn in part from Blight, David and the Yale and Slavery Research Project, Yale and Slavery: A History . Yale University Press, 2024.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: This finding aid was revised in 2024 to reflect research done by the Yale and Slavery Research Project and to further contextualize the creation of the Yale Civil War Memorial. During that revision, description was changed in the collection's scope and contents note to better indicate the inclusion of Yale-affiliated Union and Confederate soldiers in the memorial and provide additional information about the committee and subject headings were added. Previous versions of this finding aid may be available. Please contact Beinecke Library for details. If you have questions or comments about these revisions, please contact Beinecke Library. For more information on reparative archival description at Yale, see Yale's Statement on Harmful Language in Archival Description.

corpname.part: Yale University. Committee on the Civil War Memorial

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The correspondence between the members of the committee involved issues such as placement of the memorial, sketches for its completion, and the inscription. Much discussion is given over to the placement and wording of the inscription, especially concerning the title of the War. The miscellaneous correspondence is involved mostly with identifying and researching the names of Yale-affiliated Union and Confederate soldiers, for inclusion in the memorial.
